title: Crucifixion of Jesus of Nazareth
summary: In the early 30s of the first century CE, during the prefecture of Pontius Pilate in the Roman province of Judaea and probably at the Passover of 30 or 33, the Galilean teacher Jesus of Nazareth was arrested in Jerusalem, examined by the Jewish high priest Caiaphas, condemned by Pilate, and executed by Roman crucifixion outside the walls of the city at a place called Golgotha. The event is almost universally accepted as historical by ancient historians, secular and religious alike; almost every other detail is contested. Around it grew the movement that in three centuries would become the state religion of the Roman Empire.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Judaea into which Jesus was born, around 4 BCE, was a Roman client kingdom under Herod the Great. After Herod's death in that year the kingdom was divided among his sons, and in 6 CE the region of Judaea proper, including Jerusalem, was placed under direct Roman rule as a minor province of prefects reporting to the imperial legate of Syria at Antioch. The population was overwhelmingly Jewish and organised around the Second Temple in Jerusalem, rebuilt on a monumental scale by Herod between 20 BCE and 63 CE. A hereditary priesthood, headed by the high priest (kohen gadol), controlled the sacrificial cult; alongside them, and often in tension with them, competed the movements later Jewish tradition labels Pharisees, Sadducees, Essenes and Zealots.

Roman government sat lightly on the surface but heavily on the pocket. Prefects, resident at the coastal city of Caesarea Maritima, came up to Jerusalem for the great festivals with reinforcements to prevent unrest; they controlled the appointment of the high priest and took direct charge of capital jurisdiction. Pontius Pilate held the office from 26 to 36 CE, longer than most of his predecessors and successors; his rule is described in strongly critical terms by the contemporary Jewish philosopher Philo of Alexandria and by the historian Josephus, both of whom record incidents in which Pilate provoked riots by inserting images of the emperor into the city or by expropriating temple funds for aqueduct construction.

Jesus of Nazareth, according to the canonical Gospels (Mark, probably written around 70 CE, and Matthew, Luke and John of the following decades), was a Galilean carpenter's son who, in adulthood, was baptised by John the Baptist in the Jordan and began a public ministry of teaching, healing and exorcism in Galilee and neighbouring regions. He gathered a core group of twelve disciples, chose Simon Peter as their leader, and used the parables of the kingdom of God as his central rhetorical form. He seems to have been about thirty at the start of his ministry, and to have taught for perhaps three years, moving between the fishing villages of the Sea of Galilee, the Decapolis, and the north as far as Caesarea Philippi.

On the non-Christian evidence, the Roman historian Tacitus, writing his Annals about 116 CE, records that Christus had suffered the extreme penalty during the reign of Tiberius at the hands of one of our procurators, Pontius Pilatus (Annals 15.44). The Jewish historian Flavius Josephus, in his Antiquities of the Jews (93 or 94 CE), preserves a briefer notice (the Testimonium Flavianum, 18.63 to 64) that Pilate, upon the accusation of the leading men among us, had condemned him to the cross. Both passages have been subject to extensive text-critical analysis. The Testimonium as it stands contains obvious Christian interpolations, but a shorter version, without them, is regarded by most scholars as authentically Josephan. Combined, the ancient non-Christian evidence is thin but sufficient: a Jewish teacher named Jesus of Nazareth was executed by the Roman prefect of Judaea in the early first century.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
According to the four canonical Gospels, whose accounts overlap heavily on the outline of the last days and diverge on details, Jesus travelled from Galilee up to Jerusalem for the festival of Passover, riding into the city on a donkey to demonstrations of popular acclaim. At the Temple he overturned the tables of the moneychangers who exchanged coin for the half-shekel Temple tax, an act the Synoptic Gospels place a few days before his arrest and the Gospel of John places at the beginning of his ministry. Modern scholarship generally accepts a Temple confrontation of some kind as the immediate trigger for the priestly decision to move against him.

On the Thursday evening he ate a final meal with his disciples in an upper room in the city, remembered in Christian liturgy as the Last Supper and by the Synoptic Gospels as a Passover seder. After the meal he went with his closest disciples to a garden called Gethsemane on the western slope of the Mount of Olives, across the Kidron valley from the Temple. There he was arrested by a party sent from the high priest, guided, according to all four Gospels, by his own disciple Judas Iscariot.

He was taken first to the house of the former high priest Annas and then to that of the reigning high priest Joseph Caiaphas. A hearing before some form of Sanhedrin followed that night. The exact charge is variously reported: blasphemy in Mark and Matthew, on the ground that Jesus had claimed to be the Messiah and Son of God; a threat to destroy the Temple; sedition. At dawn on Friday he was handed over to Pilate at the praetorium, which was probably the fortress Antonia adjoining the Temple, though tradition also identifies Herod's palace west of the city.

Pilate, according to all four Gospels, examined Jesus, was reluctant to convict, and offered the crowd the customary Passover choice between Jesus and a prisoner named Barabbas; the crowd chose Barabbas. Pilate ordered Jesus scourged and crucified. The charge nailed to the cross, in Latin, Greek and Aramaic, was IESVS NAZARENVS REX IVDAEORVM, Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews, the Roman legal formula for the reason for execution. He was led out through the city carrying, at least in part, the horizontal beam (patibulum) to a place called in Aramaic Golgotha, the place of a skull, a low outcrop just outside the northern wall of Herodian Jerusalem, then used as a Roman execution ground.

He was crucified between two other condemned men, probably around nine in the morning by the Gospel of Mark's third hour. Crucifixion, a Roman punishment reserved for slaves and provincial rebels, killed by a combination of blood loss, exhaustion, and eventual asphyxiation; a strong victim could last for days. Jesus died relatively quickly, in about six hours; Mark and Matthew record a great darkness over the land from noon to three, and a loud cry, Eloi, Eloi, lama sabachthani, My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (a quotation of Psalm 22), before his death. The Roman centurion on watch is reported by Mark to have said, Truly this man was a son of God. In accordance with Deuteronomic law, the bodies were not to remain on the crosses overnight. Joseph of Arimathea, described as a wealthy member of the Sanhedrin sympathetic to Jesus, obtained the body from Pilate and buried it before sunset in a new rock-cut tomb in a garden nearby, sealed with a stone.

The date is not securely fixed. The Gospels all place the death on a Friday (Preparation Day) at Passover, in the prefecture of Pilate (26 to 36 CE). Two dates fit that window and the astronomical requirement of a Passover full moon on a Friday: 7 April 30 CE and 3 April 33 CE. The majority of modern scholarship, weighing the chronology of Paul's early letters and the length of the ministry, prefers one or the other; both have serious defenders. The exact year cannot at present be settled with confidence, which is why the traditional formula around 30 CE is normally used.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
For the movement Jesus had gathered, the crucifixion was in the first instance a catastrophe: the arrest of a rural teacher in the capital, his condemnation by both Jewish and Roman authorities, and his death by the most degrading punishment the Roman state possessed. The Gospels agree that Jesus's disciples fled from Gethsemane; Peter, tradition preserved with unusual candour, denied him three times in the courtyard of the high priest's house before dawn.

Within days, the same disciples began to proclaim in Jerusalem that Jesus had been raised from the dead. Whatever the historical explanation of the empty tomb and the resurrection appearances (early Christian sources report appearances to Peter, to the Twelve, to a group of over five hundred at one time, to James the brother of the Lord, and, later, to Paul on the Damascus road), the belief that Jesus had been vindicated by God transformed his execution from a defeat into a foundation. The reconstituted disciples, now including Jesus's brother James of Jerusalem, established a Nazarene community in Jerusalem that continued to worship at the Temple, kept Jewish law, and gathered on the first day of the week to break bread in remembrance of him.

By the mid-30s the movement had begun to reach out to Greek-speaking Jews and to gentile sympathisers. The most consequential early convert, Saul of Tarsus (Paul), formerly a persecutor of the movement, established himself as the apostle to the gentiles and, from roughly 45 to 60 CE, planted communities across Asia Minor, Greece, Rome and Illyricum. His authentic letters, written in Greek to those churches, are the earliest surviving Christian documents, roughly two decades before the first Gospels, and place the crucifixion and resurrection at the centre of what he calls the good news, euangelion.

The first great external shock came in 70 CE, when the Roman legions of Titus destroyed Herod's Temple and much of Jerusalem in suppressing the First Jewish Revolt. The Jerusalem church was scattered, the Temple cult ended, and the young Christian movement's centre of gravity shifted decisively into the wider Roman Mediterranean. Sporadic Roman persecutions followed, notably under Nero (64 CE), Domitian (in the 90s), Decius (250 to 251), Valerian (257 to 260), and Diocletian's Great Persecution (303 to 311). Yet by 200 CE communities existed from Britain to Persia; by 300 CE perhaps ten per cent of the empire's population was Christian; and in 313 CE, with the Edict of Milan, the emperor Constantine and his colleague Licinius extended legal recognition. In 380 CE, under Theodosius, Nicene Christianity became the state religion.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The crucifixion is the defining event of the world's largest religion, professed by roughly a third of the human race in the twenty-first century, and it has shaped western law, art, music, moral vocabulary and calendar in ways almost too many to list. The cross itself, originally the most humiliating instrument the Roman state possessed, was adopted as the movement's central symbol from at least the second century CE; by the reign of Constantine it was displayed on Roman military standards, and it has been carried at the head of coronations, funerals, hospital dedications and battles ever since.

Within Christian devotion the events of the last week, from the entry into Jerusalem to the resurrection, are commemorated annually as Holy Week, culminating on Good Friday and Easter Sunday; the calculation of Easter's date, agreed at the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE, still governs the timing of a substantial fraction of the world's calendars, from Ethiopian to Russian Orthodox. Passion plays, from the medieval York cycle to the modern Oberammergau performances staged every ten years since 1634, have been among the most enduring traditions of European drama. In music, Bach's St Matthew Passion (1727) and St John Passion (1724) are among the summits of Western art music.

The traditional site of Golgotha and the tomb has been continuously venerated since at least the fourth century CE. The emperor Constantine's mother Helena, on pilgrimage in 326 to 328 CE, identified a Jerusalem site then covered by a temple to Venus as the location; excavation beneath the temple exposed a rock-cut tomb, and over it Constantine's architects built the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, dedicated in 335 CE. Repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt, most recently after the earthquake of 1927 and the fire of 1808, it is today shared by six Christian communions (Greek Orthodox, Roman Catholic, Armenian Apostolic, Coptic Orthodox, Ethiopian Orthodox, and Syriac Orthodox) under an arrangement, the Status Quo, dating from 1757 and 1852.

Outside the church, the crucifixion has been endlessly reworked by artists, from the Rabbula Gospels of 586 CE and the Ottonian ivories of the tenth century, through Grunewald's Isenheim Altarpiece of 1516 and Velazquez's Cristo crucificado of 1632, to Salvador Dali's Christ of Saint John of the Cross of 1951. Historically the event is one of the most heavily debated in the ancient record. Its bare occurrence is accepted by almost all scholars, its precise year is not yet settled, and its meaning remains what it has been from the first Easter morning: the founding disagreement of Western religious history.

worldContext.text: The Roman Empire in 30 CE was in the sixteenth year of the reign of the emperor Tiberius, who had retired since 26 CE to Capri, leaving day-to-day government of the city of Rome to the praetorian prefect Lucius Aelius Sejanus. It was a period of political trials in the Senate, monumental public building along the Bay of Naples, and generally quiet frontiers. The empire ran from Britain (still unconquered) and the Rhine in the north-west to the Euphrates in the east; its total population, at Augustus's census, has been estimated at about sixty million.

In China the Xin dynasty of the usurper Wang Mang (r. 9 to 23 CE) had collapsed in a wave of peasant rebellion (notably the Red Eyebrows) and civil war. In 25 CE a member of the deposed Liu family, Liu Xiu, had restored the Han dynasty as its first Eastern Han emperor Guangwu (r. 25 to 57 CE), moving the capital from Chang'an to Luoyang. His early reign was still consolidating power against rival warlords in Sichuan and the north-west; the classic Eastern Han administration was taking shape.

In north-western India the Indo-Scythian (Saka) satraps ruled at Taxila and Mathura, while the Indo-Parthian house of Gondophares was consolidating its hold on Gandhara; Christian legend, preserved in the third-century Acts of Thomas, would in later centuries associate the apostle Thomas with the court of a Gondophares. In central India the Shatavahana dynasty was consolidating power in the Deccan, and in the far south the early Cheras, Cholas and Pandyas were participating in a growing maritime trade with the Roman world.

In Mesoamerica the great city of Teotihuacan in the Basin of Mexico was rising; the Pyramid of the Sun, some 65 metres high, was in its early phases of construction. In the Maya lowlands the Late Preclassic was giving way to the Early Classic, with major building at Tikal, Uaxactun and El Mirador. In the Andes the Moche culture on the north Peruvian coast was consolidating along the Chicama and Moche valleys, and the Nazca to the south were beginning the vast geoglyphs in the Pampa de Nazca.

Egypt had been a Roman province since 30 BCE, ruled by a prefect of equestrian rank reporting directly to the emperor; the province was a major granary of Rome. Further south, the Meroitic kingdom of Kush continued its royal cemeteries at Meroe and had, after the campaigns of Publius Petronius against the kandake Amanirenas in the 20s BCE, established a stable frontier at Hiera Sykaminos. Along the North African coast the client kingdom of Mauretania under Ptolemy of Mauretania continued, though within a decade it would be annexed by the emperor Caligula.

In the western Pacific ancestral Polynesian societies were consolidating in Tonga, Samoa and Fiji, developing the chiefly hierarchies, deep-sea canoe technology and horticultural repertoire that would in later centuries carry their descendants east to the Society and Marquesas Islands and, in due course, to Hawaii and Aotearoa. In Australia, Aboriginal populations continued the world's oldest unbroken cultural tradition across a continent of widely varying environments.

figures.name: Jesus of Nazareth
figures.role: Jewish teacher and healer from Galilee, b. c. 4 BCE, executed by crucifixion in Jerusalem under Pontius Pilate at roughly age 33

figures.name: Pontius Pilate
figures.role: Roman prefect (governor) of Judaea 26 to 36 CE, who tried and condemned Jesus

figures.name: Joseph Caiaphas
figures.role: Jewish high priest 18 to 36 CE, who presided over the nocturnal hearing that handed Jesus to Pilate

figures.name: Simon Peter
figures.role: Fisherman of Bethsaida, leader of the twelve disciples, later the central figure of the early Jerusalem church

figures.name: Paul of Tarsus
figures.role: Hellenistic Jewish Pharisee who, after a conversion experience in the mid-30s CE, spread the movement to gentiles across the eastern Mediterranean

images.caption: Cristo crucificado, by Diego Velazquez, c. 1632, oil on canvas. Museo del Prado, Madrid. One of the most celebrated European depictions of the crucifixion.
images.credit: Diego Velazquez (1599 to 1660), via Wikimedia Commons
